Thank you. Uh, Tēnā koutou and, and thank you for inviting us this
morning. I'm the acting Secretary and Chief Executive, Suzanne
Sturge. And today, I'm joined by the rest of the MBIE senior leadership
team, and the general managers from the areas that you have indicated you'd like us
to focus on. With me at the table currently is the Deputy Secretary for Corporate
and Digital Shared Services, Richard Griffiths-
Morning.
And on my left, the Chief Financial Officer, Paul Helm.
The other members will introduce themselves as they come up to the table.
Firstly, this morning, I would like to acknowledge our former chief executive,
Carolyn Tremain. Carolyn left MBIE in late September after
eight years as our chief executive.
Carolyn made an enormous contribution to MBIE and the wider public service.
And her leadership has been instrumental in guiding MBIE through
complex challenges and delivering outcomes in support of strengthening New
Zealand's economy and our communities.
Her commitment to public service values and her ability to foster collaboration
across government made her an outstanding leader.
I recognize that at this review this morning, we have big shoes to
fill, um, but look forward with the outstanding help of the team to
answering your questions this morning.
Now, MBIE's focus remains on supporting the delivery of government priorities and
delivering outstanding services to the public.
As an organization, we are right-sized with high-performing people
delivering value-for-money services.
Our main responsibilities span 19 interconnected ministerial
portfolios with support for several more across three main funding
areas. We also have responsibility for 17 regulatory systems
with considerable variation in scope, function, and
risk. These systems underpin economic activity and
growth and provide safety and certainty to people, businesses, and
the country as a whole. In early 2025, the government
launched Going for Growth, and we have been working at pace in
support of this priority across its five pillars, developing
talent, competitive business settings, promoting global trade and
investment, innovation, technology and science, and infrastructure for
growth. This includes delivering a broad suite of
actions from immigration and workforce initiatives to infrastructure
acceleration, investment attraction, and even science
reforms. As an organization, MBIE's efficiency initiatives
focused on structural simplification, reducing organizational
layers, and consolidating functions to streamline our decision-making.
Strong fiscal discipline drove cost savings in such areas as
contractors and consultants, property rationalization, as well
as overall operational efficiencies.
Savings include a departmental baseline reduction of $56 million
and non-departmental savings of $152 million
achieved through ending and scaling back programs that were no longer aligned with
the government's priorities. A major emphasis of our work in MBIE
is ensuring that we are well-placed for the future with our investment
in digital transformation. This includes automation and
AI tools to improve our productivity and reduce administrative
overheads. These changes were underpinned by our refreshed
strategic framework, ensuring alignment with government priorities while building
capability and resilience in our workforce.
Since we last met the committee, we have also delivered
158 Cabinet papers, contributed to the introduction of 12 government
bills to Parliament, and delivered over 4,000
briefings. I also want to acknowledge some of the key items
from the government priorities that our people have successfully achieved this
year. These include actions as part of Going for Growth.
In support of developing talent, our actions include adjusting the immigration
settings to attract skilled workers and regional investment to grow regional
economies by improving resilience and increasing
productivity.In order to improve our competitive business settings, we have
progressed reforms to the Holidays Act and Employment Relations Act, progressed
legislation to streamline financial services regulation, and conducted
reviews of the electricity, banking, and the grocery sectors.
The focus on promotion of global trade investment saw us supporting the
establishment of investment... Invest New Zealand as a one-stop shop for
foreign direct investment, and developing the tourism growth roadmap to double
tourism export value by 2034. The innovation,
technology, and science pillar, all about connecting science and technology systems
to business needs and enabling innovation across our economy.
And we have supported reform of the science system to create
four new public research organizations and established the
Prime Minister's Science Innovation and Technology Advisory Council.
In support of infrastructure for growth, our actions included facilitating a
resilient energy system, approving investments through the Regional and
Infrastructure Fund, and supporting the streamlining of building consents and
the use of overseas building products to lower costs and speed up
delivery. We also supported the delivery of major
regulatory changes, which included initiating consultation on the Holidays Act
reform and the clarification of contractor employment status, as
well as progressing regulatory changes to remove the GE ban and enable safe use of
gene technology. In our energy and resources area, we supported the
Offshore Renewable Energy Bill, the Crown Minerals Amendment Bill, and the Minerals
Strategy under review of the electricity market performance and provided
advice on reforms.
Finally, (laughs) I'd like to highlight some of the achievements of our service
delivery functions. We continue to focus on delivering
high-quality, value-for-money services for our customers.
Our customer satisfaction exceeded 75% across more than
20 services, including tenancy, dispute resolution,
and immigration.
In the last year, we handled over 1.3 million calls
in our service centers with wait times under our five-minute
target, a result I know that many of my private sector
colleagues would be envious of. We've achieved our visa
processing efficiency targets across student visa, work
visa, and employer accreditation applications, and
97% of Migrant Exploitation Protection
visas are assessed and completed within five
days. Even earlier this week, the Tenancy Bond
Services Team introduced a new IT platform, replacing our
old 25-year-old Bond 2000 paper-based
system. Not only does it support pet bonds, but enables
faster service for landlords, for tenants, and for our staff.
I'm really excited about the achievement of this major platform, so hopefully
you'll give us some opportunity later to share more on that.
We've also strengthened our compliance activities across all our regulatory
systems, and the teams are working really hard to ensure that we have a
fair playing field for everyone in New Zealand.
The quality of work that MBIE has done this year and continues to do is testament
to our fantastic people. I know that our people have worked
incredibly hard and at peace once again this year.
So I and the senior leadership team would like to thank them for all that they
have done this year in support of our purpose to grow New Zealand for all.
We are very proud of what they have achieved.
Thank you very much for your indulgence, and we're happy to answer all your
questions.

Okay, okay, let them... Yeah, just, just Andy, yeah.
Yeah. So, so, uh, they, they were, as we understand it, unaware that
these products could contain these kinds of contaminants.
So as I understand it, uh, neither WorkSafe nor Customs have taken
enforcement action as yet. Um, it would, you know...
Uh, I'll leave that, um, to them as to, to answer the questions about when and how
they, they might decide to do that within their, within their particular
legislative settings. Um, so
I, I think, again, um,
ultimately, uh, consumers should be approaching their supplier
in the first instance, asking them to take responsibility for, uh,
either, uh, you know, replacing the products, uh,
providing a refund, and taking what other, what other, um,
action that the supplier might be...
you know, the consumer might think that they're responsible for.
For example, cleanup costs. So if the supplier isn't
providing the redress that the consumer thinks is appropriate in that case, then
they can take that to the disputes tribunal.
And so they can look at costs of, uh, I think up to $30,000
at the moment. And then beyond that, um, there are obviously
the, the courts as well that, uh, that this can be, um, prosecuted
through.
Okay. Uh, probably from Gillian this-
I, I just wanted to understand the, the last question I didn't get in was, um,
on a different topic, sorry. But the, back to the AI and, uh, someone
mentioned that there's some work going on with small business, for assistance for
small business-
Yep. ... for their adaptational training. I just wanted to know a bit more about that.
Is it a specific program? What stage is it at? When's it gonna be rolled out?
Yes, it has been. Uh, uh, is a, a specific part of the regional business partner
network. Oh God, Andy, you probably know about it-
Yeah,
that's right. So, so- ... as well as I do. Um, but it has been a trailer under EI?
I-
No, it's-
Th- that's right. So, um, so I look after small business, uh-
(laughs)
... policy as well. So, um, we're right in the process of, uh, of
rolling out a, a trial for, um, our regional business partners to be
able to work with businesses to, uh, provide, uh, co-funding
for particular, um, AI trials within, within businesses
in, um, uh, in regions. So, so that's something that, that we're just
rolling out now. And, and we'll, we'll have a look at how that goes for, for firms
and then, uh, you know, make a call about how, uh, you know, further,
further, um-
Mm-hmm.
... funding in, within the regional business partner program is, uh, is
appropriated down the track.
And, and so the regional business partners, would they identify with the software
or whatever that... Do they choose that or, or how much...
Is it off the shelf or how does that-
Yeah. So, so my understanding is that, um, is the way that it would work was that,
um, there would be... that, yeah, the, the regional business partners themselves
would identify appropriate suppliers in the region of, of this kind of
advice, and then be essentially matching up businesses that, that have a
particular, uh, that have a particular need or have a particular interest,
um, that they're seeking through, um, to develop through AI and
then making that match and then ............................
Okay. And is there any ability or inclination for the procurement process
to identify New Zealand providers that could be utilized with a view
to how we're developing our own internal infrastructure for AI?
Um, I'm not... Look, I'm not, I'm not sure it favors any particular...
I mean, obviously, the, the, the agent in, in New Zealand would
be a, uh... you know, would be an New Zealand agent, but the software and
AI tools that they might apply-
Yeah.
Um, I don't think we've got any particular settings around that.
Obviously, a lot of the, uh, a lot of the AI tools that have been
developing, um, a- around the world are, you know, global
institutions. But obviously there's other options out there.
Yeah.
So that's something that we could think about.
And, and with your... Sorry, final question.
Um, i- is there any way you'd be formulating kind of
useful, um, feedback on what worked, what didn't-
Yes.
... what they liked-
Absolutely.
... yeah, all, all the typical stuff you would usually do, but-
And there's no irony in the fact that we might be using some AI (laughs) to analyze
some of those insights.
But yes, we are taking, um... we are actually using AI to extract what we're
learning from those mentors and the regional business partner network to give us
some insights to use into the next phase.
So yeah, as well as, as helping other businesses use it, we're using it ourselves
to actually further the use of AI.
One, one of the prohibiting factors sometimes with small business owners would be
you're a, um, owner-operator who's already pretty snowed under with-
Yeah.
... meeting day-to-day business requirements.
Um, there seems to me to be an opportunity there with young people
who are more digitally native coming up through.
Is there any view of how regional business partners would be
utilizing, um, an emerging younger workforce to be
partnering up with small business alongside with some training to increase
productivity and opportunities for, for, for, um,
raising wages for, for people coming up through?
Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. Yeah, it's a good question and I, and I think, um, you know,
that's, that's something that we're thinking about in the, in the context of the
manufacturing portfolio as well, is how we draw more young people into
these kinds of roles which, um, you know, historically manufacturing roles have
been sort of considered to be, you know, um, quite sort of
task-oriented where, whereas actually there is quite a lot of technology coming
through, uh, in a, in a lot of these jobs.
And so, um, within the, um, within the manufacturing
portfolio one of the things that we are doing is funding the, um, advancing
manufacturing Aotearoa to roll out, uh, essentially,
um, uh,
work, work schemes for... to put simple young people into, uh,
essentially, uh, educate while you, while you work scheme.
Okay.
So, we had a good trial of that last year up in Hamilton and, and the AMI are
looking at, um, at rolling that out more widely as well, um, in
the context of, of small business.
Um, I think y- one of the things with AI in, in small businesses-
Oh, so can I ask, what was the Hamilton one called? What was the name of...
Did it have a name? The Hamilton one you just referred to?
Yes, I'm, I'm struggling to remember the name of it.
I'm,
We'll come back to you with the name.
I can come back to you. I can come back to you on that.
Um-Uh, I should know the answer to that, but, um, but yeah, we can
come back to you about that. But in the- in the context of, um, of small business,
I think that there is a lot to be gained, um, by, you know,
the really small businesses. You know, the ones that- that are essentially,
um, you know, full-time, uh, full-time running the
show. Um, you know, things... th- th- there are
off-the-shelf, uh, sort of AI tools that they can pick up relatively
cheaply. So, I think one of the things that we'd like to understand through the
regional business partner trial is who gains most, um,
out of- out of some of the more sophisticated tools that AI can
provide. And those might be some of the sort of, you know, five to
20 FTEs as opposed to-
Mm-hmm.
... uh, as opposed to zero FTEs or- or one FTE.
Those people can probably get quite a lot of, um, you know, quite a lot of
traction just by, you know, getting s- a subscription to one of the,
um, accounting software services or something like that-
Mm-hmm.
... that employ AI in their service already.
Mm-hmm.
Um, so that's one of the things that we're looking at as well.
Um, part of that will also... Um, our research as part of this program into what's
the barriers to adoption for AI.
Mm-hmm. So, we'll get some insight not only in what are the uses and what are the benefits,
but what are the barriers for small business in particular entering into that?
Is it, you know, is it fear of technology? Is it skill?
Is it just time? Yeah.
Is it understanding the breadth of the market?
So, we'll get some insight into that as well.
Okay.
And that will help us tailor-
What's the rough timeframe
for that? All right. No, no, members. We don't have time for any
more questions.
We don't have the time. Sorry. (laughs) Uh, any further questions can be asked later on.
(laughs)
Uh, so we'll have to suspend this until 1:20. Uh, thank you so much.
We'll see you again at 1:20.
Right. Good morning.
Thank you. Thank you very much, Chair.
So, we move into private session. Thank you.
It'll take me a few minutes to make up all my
papers. (laughs) ???
I'm just gonna...
Yeah.
... retrieve them.
